lesson:
A mash-up of あっち (over there) and こっち (over here), smashed together with neither direction winning. There and here and there and here — all over the place. あちこち探さがした (I searched all over). あちこちに花はなが咲いている (flowers are blooming here and there).

The word has an aimless, scattered quality. It's not a systematic search or an organized tour — it's bouncing around from place to place without a fixed plan. 旅行いりょこうであちこち回った (I went around various places on my trip). 体からだのあちこちが痛いたい (various parts of my body hurt) — not just one spot, but scattered all over.

You might also hear あちらこちら, the polite full form. Same meaning, just longer and more formal.

The polite version of そっち. Where そっち points casually toward the listener's vicinity, そちら does the same thing with a bow. そちらはどうですか (how are things on your end?). そちらに届とどきましたか (did it arrive there?).

The real reason to learn そちら is its use as a polite "you." Japanese avoids direct second-person pronouns in formal contexts — saying あなた to a customer or superior is awkward at best. そちら sidesteps the problem: そちらのお名前まえなまえは？ (your name?) — literally "the name of that-direction" but understood as "your name." そちらでよろしいですか (is that all right with you?). It's indirect, respectful, and useful in any customer-facing situation.

The polite form of あっち — pointing toward something far from both speaker and listener. あちらのテーブルへどうぞ (please go to that table over there). In restaurants, shops, and hotels, あちら is the word staff use to direct you.

Like そちら, あちら can refer to a person — but with distance. あちらの方かた (that person over there) is polite third-person reference. あちらは田中なかさんです (that (person) is Tanaka-san).

Together, こちら・そちら・あちら form the polite spatial trio — near me, near you, far from both. They're the versions you use in any situation where こっち・そっち・あっち would feel too rough. In a job interview, at a reception desk, in a formal email — always 〜ちら, never 〜っち.

もし is the word that opens hypotheticals — the Japanese "if" that flags what follows as uncertain or conditional. もし時間まじかんがあれば、行いきたい (if I have time, I'd like to go). もし明日にちあした雨あめだったら、中なか止とです (if it rains tomorrow, it's cancelled).

Here's the key insight: もし is technically optional. Japanese has conditional forms built into verbs — 〜ば, 〜たら, 〜なら — and those already signal "if." Adding もし is like adding "just suppose" or "what if" in English — it emphasizes the hypothetical nature and signals the listener to shift into if-mode. Without もし, the sentence still works grammatically. With it, the conditional is foregrounded.

That makes もし a framing device more than a grammatical requirement. It's especially useful in longer sentences where the conditional clause might otherwise sneak up on the listener. Think of it as a courtesy: "heads up, we're entering hypothetical territory."

うるさい starts as "noisy" and escalates fast. 外そとがうるさい (it's noisy outside) — that's the basic meaning. But うるさい！ shouted at someone means "shut up!" — one of the rudest things you can say. And 彼かれは味にうるさい means "he's picky about flavor" — not loud at all, but fussy, particular, hard to please. Three meanings, one word, enormous tonal range.

The "picky" meaning is the one that surprises learners. あの人ひとはルールにうるさい (that person is strict about rules). うちの母ははは掃除にうるさい (my mom is particular about cleaning). The logic: someone who is うるさい about something won't stop talking about it, won't let imperfections slide — they're annoyingly persistent in their standards. The noise metaphor became a metaphor for meticulousness.

Written in kanji as 五ご月つき蠅い — "May flies." Because flies buzzing around you in May are the original うるさい experience. This ateji (kanji chosen for meaning, not sound) is so perfect that it stuck.

Dictionaryうるさい
Negativeうるさくない
Pastうるさかった
Past negうるさくなかった
Adverbうるさく
て-formうるさくて

Japanese has three kanji all read かたい, and they split the concept of "hard" into three territories. 固かたい is hard in the sense of firm, solid, rigid — a 固かたい約束やくそく (a firm promise), 固かたい握手て (a firm handshake), 固かたいパン (hard bread). 硬い is physically hard like metal or stone. 堅い is hard in the sense of reliable, steady, conservative — 堅い商売う (a solid business).

In practice, 固かたい is the default and covers most situations. The distinctions matter in writing but blend together in speech, since they all sound identical.

The figurative uses are where 固かたい gets interesting. 頭あたまが固かたい (hardheaded, inflexible in thinking). 固かたい表情ひょうじょう (a stiff/tense expression). 固かたくなる (to stiffen up — from nervousness). A person who is 固かたい is formal, uptight, serious to a fault — the opposite of loose and relaxed.

Dictionaryかたい
Negativeかたくない
Pastかたかった
Past negかたくなかった
Adverbかたく
て-formかたくて

The kanji 深ふか is water (氵さんずい) on the left and a component on the right that suggests something penetrating downward. Water going deep. The physical meaning comes first: 深ふかい川かわ (a deep river), 深ふかい穴 (a deep hole), 深ふかい森 (a deep forest).

But Japanese uses 深ふかい for abstract depth just as aggressively as English does, sometimes more so. 深ふかい意味いみ (a deep meaning). 関係が深ふかい (the relationship is deep/close). 興味あじが深ふかい (deeply interested). And one that doesn't translate directly: 霧が深ふかい — fog is "deep" in Japanese, not "thick." You wade into fog the way you wade into water.

Time gets the 深ふかい treatment too. 夜よるが深ふかい (the night is deep — late at night). 深ふか夜よる (しんや, deep night — the small hours). Night isn't late; it's deep. You're far below the surface of the day.

Dictionaryふかい
Negativeふかくない
Pastふかかった
Past negふかくなかった
Adverbふかく
て-formふかくて

The kanji spell out something bizarre: 面めん (face) + 白しろい (white). A white face. The origin story: in ancient Japanese court performances, the audience's faces would go pale — literally white — with amazement or delight when something captivating happened. The white face of astonishment became the word for the thing that caused it. Whether this etymology is precisely true is debated, but it's old enough to be essentially fossilized.

面めん白しろい covers "interesting" and "funny" without distinguishing between them the way English does. この本ほんは面白しろい could mean the book is interesting or that it's amusing — or both. 面めん白しろい人ひと is an interesting/fun person. 面めん白しろくない means either "not interesting" or "I'm not amused" — and in the latter case, watch out, because it can carry an edge of displeasure.

Sound anchor: o-mo-shi-ro-i — five syllables, the longest common adjective in this list. The length itself is memorable. And the しろ in the middle is literally 白しろ (white) — the white-faced audience, frozen in fascination.

Dictionaryおもしろい
Negativeおもしろくない
Pastおもしろかった
Past negおもしろくなかった
Adverbおもしろく
て-formおもしろくて
